name: Yomi
description: Yomi, also known as Yomi-no-kuni (黄泉の国), is the land of the dead in Japanese mythology and Shinto cosmology. It is a perpetually dark and filthy realm, often depicted as being located beneath the earth, accessed through a dark and winding passage or cave. Unlike some other underworlds that offer judgment or reward, Yomi is primarily a place of stagnation and decay. Once one consumes food from Yomi, they are forever bound to it, making escape exceedingly difficult, if not impossible. The atmosphere of Yomi is oppressive, characterized by a sense of inescapable gloom and the ever-present threat of the decaying spirits that reside within. It is a place of defilement, contrasting sharply with the purity sought in Shinto rituals. The landscape itself is often described as barren and desolate, reflecting the spiritual emptiness of the dead who dwell there. Yomi serves as a stark reminder of the inevitability of death and the dangers of contact with the impure.
subtitle: The land of the dead in Japanese mythology.
location: Beneath the earth, accessed through a cave or passage.
significance: Represents the inevitable fate of death and the dangers of impurity.

keyMyths.title: Izanagi and Izanami's Descent
keyMyths.description: Izanagi, grieving the death of his wife Izanami during the birth of the fire god Kagutsuchi, descends to Yomi to retrieve her. He finds her transformed into a decaying corpse. Despite her warnings, he looks upon her, horrified by her state. Enraged, Izanami orders shikome (foul women) and yakusa-no-ikazuchi (thunder deities) to pursue him. He barely escapes, sealing the entrance to Yomi with a large boulder.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the Seasons
keyMyths.description: While not directly about Yomi itself, the separation of Izanagi and Izanami and the sealing of the entrance to Yomi is often interpreted as the mythological origin of death and the separation of the world of the living from the world of the dead. This separation contributes to the cyclical nature of life and death, and, by extension, the seasons.

keyMyths.title: The Pursuit by the Shikome
keyMyths.description: During Izanagi's escape from Yomi, Izanami sends shikome, hideous women, to chase after him. Izanagi throws down his headdress, which transforms into grapes, and his comb, which transforms into bamboo shoots, to distract them and slow their pursuit. This highlights the desperate measures one must take to escape the clutches of Yomi and its inhabitants.

sources.author: Chamberlain, Basil Hall
sources.description: An English translation of the Kojiki, one of the earliest written records of Japanese mythology and history. Provides a detailed account of the myth of Izanagi and Izanami's descent to Yomi.
sources.text: An English translation of the Kojiki, one of the earliest written records of Japanese mythology and history. Provides a detailed account of the myth of Izanagi and Izanami's descent to Yomi.

sources.author: Aston, W.G.
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another important source for Japanese mythology. Offers a different perspective on the events surrounding Izanagi and Izanami.
sources.text: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another important source for Japanese mythology. Offers a different perspective on the events surrounding Izanagi and Izanami.

sources.author: Philippi, Donald L.
sources.description: A modern translation and annotation of the Kojiki, offering insights into the cultural and historical context of the myths.
sources.text: A modern translation and annotation of the Kojiki, offering insights into the cultural and historical context of the myths.
